name: Roseto Effect
what_it_demonstrates: Intense social cohesion can override standard cardiovascular risk factors to produce dramatically lower heart disease mortality — and the effect erodes within a generation once cohesion breaks down, confirming the causal role of the social input rather than any genetic or dietary confounder.
narrative: Roseto, Pennsylvania was a small Italian-American immigrant community with unusually strong intergenerational family structures, tight social cohesion, and community rituals through the 1950s-60s. Stewart Wolf and colleagues documented roughly half the heart disease mortality of surrounding communities despite comparable standard risk factors (diet, smoking, occupational exposures). As the younger generation assimilated into mainstream American individualism across the 1970s-80s, the social cohesion eroded and the mortality advantage disappeared on schedule. The Roseto effect is a natural experiment for DA2/DC1: strong social scaffolding can suppress the cardiovascular consequences of allostatic load, and its removal reveals the baseline.
effect_size: Roughly 50% lower heart disease mortality vs comparable communities during the cohesion period; effect disappeared within one generation of cohesion erosion.
primary_metric_display: ~50% lower heart disease mortality during cohesion era

name: The Roseto Effect
what_it_demonstrates: Italian-American town had half the heart disease of neighbors — not from diet, exercise, or genetics, but from social cohesion. When the community dissolved, the protection vanished.
narrative: Roseto, Pennsylvania was studied for 50 years (1935-1985). Despite worse risk factors — higher fat diets, similar smoking rates — the town had significantly lower myocardial infarction mortality than neighboring Bangor. The protective factor was social architecture: multigenerational households, community rituals, an egalitarian ethos that discouraged ostentatious wealth display. When younger generations adopted American individualism — separate homes, status competition, weakened ties — heart disease mortality rose to match the national average. Same genetics. Same geography. Different social inputs. Different outputs. (Egolf et al. 1992, American Journal of Public Health; Wolf & Bruhn 1993, The Roseto Story.)
effect_size: 50% lower myocardial infarction mortality during cohesive period (1955-1965); rose to match national average after social dissolution (by 1985). Egolf et al. 1992.
primary_metric_display: 50% lower MI mortality during social cohesion; matched national average after dissolution
